attributes.stem: A 21-year-old, Para 0, is being assessed in the second stage of labour. Her antenatal course was uncomplicated, with a normal oral glucose tolerance test. The first stage of labour was prolonged, with an epidural and syntocinon being utilised for augmentation of labour. She has now been pushing for 90 minutes. It is observed that with pushing the head becomes visible but then retracts back against the perineum into the vaginal canal. Help is called for and the woman is told to stop pushing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Perform a pelvic examination and attempt to elevate the head
Reposition the woman onto all fours
Reposition the woman onto the end of the bed and get her to flex and abduct her hips
attributes.explanation:
This is a presentation of shoulder dystocia, a bony obstruction where the anterior shoulder is impacted behind the pubic symphysis. It is an obstetric emergency, and the priority of management is to call for help and to tell the woman to stop pushing.

The first manoeuvre to perform is McRobert's: this involves getting the mother to hyperflex her hips (knees to chest position). This will straighten the lumbosacral angle, rotate the pelvis and increase the anterior-posterior diameter of the pelvis, correcting approximately 50% of shoulder dystocias. It is important to provide gentle axial traction on the fetus once in this position. An episiotomy can be considered but this is to ensure adequate space for the internal manoeuvres, not correct the bony obstruction. 

Systematic progression through the manoeuvres is key- suprapubic pressure (decreases the fetal shoulder diameter and rotates the anterior shoulder) followed by internal manoeuvres.  Current teaching is to proceed to delivery of the posterior arm which reduces the diameter of the fetal shoulders. Additional internal manoeuvres include Rubins II or Woods Screw/reverse Woods Screw. If the shoulder dystocia persists despite trying all these manoeuvres, the woman can be repositioned onto all fours and the manoeuvres repeated systematically. Third-line manoeuvres include a deliberate clavicle fracture, pubic symphysiotomy and the Zavanelli manoeuvre where the fetus is pushed back into the birth canal and the mother is taken for an emergency Caesarean section. These options should be considered carefully to avoid unnecessary maternal morbidity and mortality. 

·       Maternal risks include postpartum haemorrhage and obstetric anal sphincter injury.

·       Neonatal risks include brachial plexus injury, humerus/clavical fractures, as well as NICU admission for management of pneumothoraces and hypoxic brain injury.
learningPoints: The McRobert's manoeuvre is the first action to take in shoulder dystocia.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Shoulder Dystocia, Green-top Guideline No. 42. Second edition. March 2012.
Thorogood C, Hendy S. Life-threatening emergencies. In: Pairman S, Pincombe J, thorogood C, Tracy S, editors. Midwifery preparation for practice. Sydney: Churchill Livingstone; 2006. p. 756-802.
